nodes.body:
Would doing something like this be possible? Just checking if we can set EKS version at one place and keep kubectl version synchronized.

```suggestion
  default = "https://s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/amazon-eks/${var.eks_version}/2023-08-16/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
```

I think I just copy-pasta'd this from the existing script, I can't find a download url that only has `major.minor` in it, so we would still need the patch version. I can add logic to just extract `major.minor` from the full version I guess.
Is there a dep that needs Go 1.19? If I recall, we've been installing 1.17 since `dino` hasn't been updated to a newer Go version.
1.17 isn't available in linuxbrew anymore, so had to update unfortunately. Ideally we will move away from installing language runtimes using brew.
Ah, ok. Thank you for the update.
Does Homebrew need the `packaging` library? I'm somewhat hesitant on passing `--break-system-packages` when building the AMI.
Not homebrew specifically, but ansible needs it for running pip install. I think this is probably a bug with brew ansible, I can add a comment.
Actually, just removed this since it's not necessary anymore.

This may be similar to those Python packages we used to install on Mac and are not really used. Have you tried removing this block?

I'm guessing that we'd only need to pass `--break-system-packages` for "developer preferences": https://github.com/cerebrotech/domino/blob/develop/dev/setup/ansible/roles/common/tasks/user_preferences.yml#L42-L56. That playbook is still referencing Python 3.10. It's probably not used by anyone at the moment.

I've been a bit reluctant to remove this, since it is part of the "interface" that dev-v2 is offering e.g. are there scripts that depend on pyyaml implicitly? On the other hand it's time we stopped doing that and made people set up proper virtual envs, so I'll yeet this like we did locally.
Are these messages written to a log file for every node startup and shutdown, or are they mainly for reference if someone ran the script manually?

The script is run by systemd, which has it's own logging mechanism. I was mostly using it as a reference when testing, since it's hard to see what is happening when a node is restarting. I can remove if you want?
No biggie, we can keep it. I was mainly curious if we'd have to check some log file in case the script doesn't run.
